subject.name: English
subject.description: Workplace communication, technical writing and reading for meaning.

quizzes.title: Communication on Site
quizzes.description: Writing that gets read, understood and acted on.

questions.text: Which opening is most appropriate for an email to a customer you have not met?
questions.explain: Named and formal. 'To whom it may concern' is for when you genuinely don't know the recipient.

questions.text: Which sentence is written in the passive voice?
options: The valve was fitted by the plumber.
The plumber is fitting the valve.
questions.explain: Passive puts the object first and the actor last (or drops them). Method statements often use it — but active is clearer for instructions.
Maintenance — 'ten' in the middle, not 'tain'. It's on nearly every job sheet you'll write.

questions.text: In a method statement, why should each step begin with a verb (e.g. 'Isolate the supply')?
options: It makes each step an unambiguous instruction someone can follow and tick off
questions.explain: Imperative verbs remove ambiguity. 'The supply should ideally be isolated' leaves room for someone not to do it.
Same sound, different spelling and meaning. Spellcheck will not catch these for you.

questions.text: Put this customer complaint response into a logical order.
items: Thank the customer for getting in touch
Acknowledge the specific problem they reported
Explain what caused it, in plain language
State what you will do to put it right and by when
Give a direct contact for any further questions
questions.explain: Acknowledge before explaining. Jumping straight to the cause reads as defensive.
